title: Recruiting Families Using Data Act of 2025
short_title: Recruiting Families Using Data Act of 2025
stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.
plain_summary: This bill requires states to make detailed plans to find and keep more foster and adoptive families. The plans must include input from families and young people who have experience with foster care. States will need to work on keeping foster children in stable homes, placing more kids with relatives, and finding diverse families that match the backgrounds of children who need homes. States must also report to Congress each year about how many foster families they have, what problems families face, and how they are working to recruit different types of families.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.
plain_keywords: keeping kids in foster care safe

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Recruiting Families Using Data Act of two thousand twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2 State Plan Amendment
sections.text: This section changes an existing law called the Social Security Act. It requires states to create a special plan called a family partnership plan. This plan must help states find, recruit, check, license, support, and keep foster and adoptive families. The plan must be created by working with birth parents, relatives, foster families, adoptive families, service providers, and young people who have lived in foster care. The plan must explain how the state will find and help relatives or other people connected to children as possible homes. It must also explain how the state will create special recruitment plans for every child who needs a foster or adoptive family. The plan must describe how children and teens will be included in efforts to find them families. The state must use information and data to set goals, check progress, reduce the number of children in group homes, help more children find permanent families, make placements more stable, place more children with relatives, and better match foster and adoptive families with children who need them. The state must also support or create advisory boards made up of foster families to help improve recruitment and keeping families involved. Each year, the state must collect and report information about how many licensed foster families they have, including families who want to adopt. They must report the backgrounds and characteristics of these families, how many are not being used and why, and information about children placed in group homes both in their state and in other states. The plan must include yearly updates with feedback from foster and adoptive parents and youth about licensing, training, support, and reasons why parents stop fostering or why adoptions fail. The plan must also include a yearly report about challenges or barriers to recruiting and licensing families who share the same racial and ethnic backgrounds as children in foster care, and what the state is doing to fix these problems. These changes will start on October first, two thousand twenty-six. However, if a state needs to pass new laws to meet these requirements, they have until the first day of the first quarter after their next regular legislative session ends to comply.

sections.title: Section 3 Inclusion of Information on Foster and Adoptive Families in Annual Child Welfare Outcomes Report to Congress
sections.text: This section changes the yearly report that goes to Congress about child welfare outcomes. Starting with the report for fiscal year two thousand twenty-five and every year after, the report must include new information. It must have data from each state about the number, backgrounds, and characteristics of foster and adoptive families in that state. It must also include the number of potential foster and adoptive families that are not being used and explain why they are not being used. The report must summarize the challenges and barriers that foster and adoptive parents face, including problems with recruitment, getting licensed, staying involved, and keeping families together. This summary should be based on surveys that states conduct with foster and adoptive parents to understand why parents stop fostering, why adoptions fail, or why foster and adoptive families have difficulties. The report must also summarize the challenges and barriers that states face when trying to recruit families that have the same racial and ethnic backgrounds as the children in foster care in their state, and what efforts states are making to overcome these barriers.
